section.title: The Treaty at Beersheba
section.sectionRationale: Right after God has vindicated Sarah and provided for Hagar's son, a pagan king comes seeking a treaty because he can see that God is with Abraham. The wandering sojourner secures a foothold in the land, a well and a name, and worships the Eternal God.
section.thesis: The nations begin to recognise that God is with Abraham; the pilgrim who owns nothing makes peace, secures a well, and calls on the LORD as the Everlasting God who keeps His promises.
section.frameNote: Three scenes: Abimelech asks for an oath; the dispute over a seized well; peace, a name for the place, and worship at Beersheba.

units.label: Abimelech seeks an oath
units.note: God is with you in all that you do

verses.text: At that time Abimelech and Phicol the commander of his army said to Abraham, “God is with you in all that you do.

Now, therefore, {{swear|swear}} to me here before God that you will not deal falsely with me or my children or descendants. Show to me and to the country in which you reside the same kindness that I have shown to you.”
But when Abraham complained to Abimelech about a well that Abimelech’s servants had seized,
Abimelech replied, “I do not know who has done this. You did not tell me, so I have not heard about it until today.”
So Abraham brought sheep and cattle and gave them to Abimelech, and the two men made a {{covenant|covenant}}.
Abraham separated seven ewe lambs from the flock,
and Abimelech asked him, “Why have you set apart these seven ewe lambs?”
He replied, “You are to accept the seven ewe lambs from my hand as my witness that I dug this well.”
So that place was called {{beersheba|Beersheba}}, because it was there that the two of them swore an oath.
After they had made the covenant at Beersheba, Abimelech and Phicol the commander of his army got up and returned to the land of the Philistines.
And Abraham planted a tamarisk tree in Beersheba, and there he called upon the name of the LORD, {{elolam|the Eternal God}}.
And Abraham resided in the land of the Philistines for a long time.

swear.gloss: To swear an oath or bind oneself by solemn promise. The verb is built on the number seven, and to swear is literally to seven oneself. Oaths in Genesis are weighty acts taken before God as witness, and here they turn a tense relationship into a lasting peace.

covenant.gloss: A binding agreement or treaty, often sealed with a sign or a sacrifice. Between Abraham and Abimelech it is a peace pact between neighbours. The same word carries the far greater bond God Himself makes with Abraham, and finally the new covenant sealed in the blood of Christ.

beersheba.gloss: A place name meaning well of seven or well of the oath. It marks both the seven lambs given as witness and the oath sworn there. Beersheba becomes a settled landmark of the patriarchs and a standing memorial that God gave Abraham a foothold in the land of promise.

elolam.gloss: A title for God meaning the Everlasting or Eternal God. Olam speaks of unbounded, age long duration. Abraham worships the God who does not change or pass away, the fitting anchor for a man who owns almost nothing but holds an everlasting promise.

basic.context: Abraham is still a resident foreigner in Canaan, owning no land. A local king, Abimelech, comes with his army commander to make a peace treaty because he can see that God blesses everything Abraham does. Along the way Abraham raises a complaint about a well his men had seized. The key question is what it looks like for God's promise to start bearing fruit even before Abraham owns the land.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a narrative about real events, not a set of commands. It shows the promise of Genesis 12, that Abraham would be a blessing to the nations, beginning to come true as a pagan king seeks him out. The interpreter should read it as one more step in God keeping His word, not as a rule for treaty making.
basic.originalLanguages: The scene turns on oaths: to swear, shaba, is tied to the number seven, and Abraham sets apart seven ewe lambs at the place he names Beersheba, the well of the oath. The two men cut a covenant, berith. At the end Abraham worships El Olam, the Everlasting God, the God whose promises outlast every human treaty.
basic.typology: Abraham the sojourner gaining a secure well and a place of worship is a small foretaste of the inheritance God has promised. It points ahead to the people of God who, though pilgrims now, will receive a lasting home. The well of living water anticipates the water Christ gives that never runs dry.
basic.christ: Abimelech recognises that God is with Abraham, an early sign of the nations being drawn to the line through which Christ will come. The Everlasting God whom Abraham calls upon is the same God fully revealed in Jesus, who makes lasting peace not by a bargain but by His own blood.
basic.god: God is faithful and present: His visible blessing on Abraham is what draws Abimelech. He is the Everlasting God, unchanging, who can be trusted to keep a promise across generations. He gives His pilgrim people footholds of grace along the way.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 12 promised blessing to the nations through Abraham, and here a king seeks him out. Hebrews 11 calls Abraham a stranger who looked for a lasting city. Isaiah 40 celebrates the Everlasting God who does not grow weary.
basic.oneStory: The wandering man of promise makes peace, secures a well, and worships the Eternal God. It is a quiet scene, but it shows God beginning to fulfil His pledge to bless the world through Abraham, the line that runs all the way to Christ.

context.literary: This closes the Isaac and Ishmael cycle of chapter 21 with a diplomatic scene at Beersheba, a place that becomes a recurring patriarchal landmark. It is told in measured dialogue and ends with a note of settled worship, a pause of peace before the great test of chapter 22.
context.historical: Abimelech is king of Gerar in the Philistine region; wells were vital and often contested property in a semi arid land, so treaties over water rights were serious business. Oaths sealed with gifts of livestock fit the customs of the ancient Near East.
context.canonical: Beersheba marks the southern edge of the land and recurs through Genesis and beyond. The title El Olam joins the growing set of names by which the patriarchs know God, each unveiling more of His character.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show God's promise beginning to bear fruit as a foreign king seeks Abraham out, and to record how Abraham gained a settled well and place of worship in the land while still a sojourner.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes a treaty and an act of worship; it does not prescribe a formula for either. The abiding truths are God's faithfulness, His visible blessing, and Abraham's habit of calling on the LORD.

debates.question: Is Abimelech's treaty a sign of God's blessing on Abraham or merely shrewd politics by a nervous neighbour?

views.label: A fruit of God's blessing
views.case: Abimelech openly says God is with you in all that you do, echoing the promise of Genesis 12; the narrator frames the whole scene as blessing recognised by the nations.

views.label: Political self interest
views.case: Abimelech acts to protect himself and his descendants from a powerful neighbour, a natural bit of statecraft that need not carry deeper theological weight.

debates.bearing: Both can be true at once: God's blessing is real, and it is precisely that visible blessing that moves a self interested king to seek peace, which is how the promise to bless the nations works out on the ground.

study.originalLanguages: The Hebrew binds the scene together through sound and number. To swear, shaba, is cousin to seven, sheva, and Abraham sets apart seven ewe lambs as a witness, naming the place Beersheba, the well of the oath or well of seven. The men cut a covenant, berith, and Abraham then calls on El Olam, the Everlasting God, contrasting a temporary human treaty with the God who endures forever.

typology.fulfillment: A pilgrim's foothold that anticipates the lasting inheritance of God's people, and the living water Christ gives that never runs dry, John 4:14.
The eternal God fully made known in the eternal Son, through whom lasting peace with God is made.

study.christ: Abimelech's confession that God is with Abraham is an early ripple of the promise that all nations will be blessed through his seed, a line that runs to Christ. Abraham worships El Olam, the Everlasting God, the same God who in Jesus makes an everlasting peace, not by a bargain over wells but by His own blood, so that pilgrims like Abraham are given a lasting home. The habit of calling on the name of the LORD, planting a tree and worshipping as a stranger in the land, foreshadows the worshipping people gathered from every nation.
study.god: God is faithful, present, and everlasting. His blessing on Abraham is so evident that a pagan king seeks a treaty; He is the God who is with His people in all they do. As El Olam He does not change or grow weary, which is exactly why His promise can be trusted across the long delay between pledge and fulfilment.

crossReferences.note: The promise that Abraham will be blessed and be a blessing to the nations; Abimelech's treaty is an early fulfilment.
Isaac makes a strikingly similar treaty with Abimelech at Beersheba, showing the promise carried to the next generation.
Abraham lived as a stranger in the land, looking for the city with foundations whose builder is God.
The LORD is the everlasting God, the Creator who does not faint or grow weary, echoing the title El Olam.

study.oneStory: The man to whom God promised a land still owns none of it, yet here a king seeks him out because God is plainly with him, and Abraham secures a well and worships the Everlasting God. It is a small, hopeful sign that the promise to bless the nations through Abraham is on the move, the promise that reaches its goal in Christ.

quiz.q: Why did Abimelech come to make a treaty with Abraham?

options.text: Because he could see that God was with Abraham in all that he did.
options.note: Abimelech says so directly in verse 22; the blessing of God is what draws him.

options.text: Because he wanted a share of Abraham's flocks and herds.
options.note: Gifts of livestock are exchanged, but the stated reason is that God was with Abraham.

options.text: Because Abraham had threatened him with war.
options.note: Abraham raises a grievance about a well, but there is no threat of war; the treaty is one of peace.

options.text: Because God commanded Abimelech to do so in a dream.
options.note: No such command is given here; Abimelech acts on what he has seen of God's blessing.

quiz.q: What does the name Beersheba signify in this passage?

options.text: Well of the oath or well of seven, marking the oath sworn and the seven lambs given as witness.
options.note: The name ties together the oath and the seven ewe lambs set apart as a witness.

options.text: Simply the place where Abraham camped for a season.
options.note: Abraham does reside there, but the name specifically commemorates the oath and the seven lambs.

options.text: The city Abraham built and fortified.
options.note: Abraham plants a tree and digs a well; he does not build a fortified city.

options.text: The name of the well Abimelech's servants had seized.
options.note: Beersheba names the place of the covenant and oath, not the seized well as such.

quiz.q: How did Abraham respond after the treaty was made?

options.text: He planted a tamarisk tree and called on the LORD, the Everlasting God.
options.note: Abraham marks the peace with an act of worship to El Olam.

options.text: He returned at once to Hebron and built an altar there.
options.note: He worships, but the text says he planted a tree at Beersheba and resided in the land, not that he returned to Hebron.

options.text: He demanded that Abimelech leave the land.
options.note: It is Abraham who resides in the land; there is no such demand.

options.text: He broke the covenant as soon as Abimelech left.
options.note: Abraham keeps the peace and settles there for a long time.

quiz.q: What does the title El Olam, the Everlasting God, emphasise here?

options.text: That God endures forever and so can be trusted to keep His promises across time.
options.note: The Everlasting God is the fitting anchor for a pilgrim holding a long delayed promise.

options.text: That God is powerful enough to win any battle.
options.note: God's power is real, but this title stresses His eternity and permanence rather than might in battle.

options.text: That God lives only in the land of Canaan.
options.note: The title speaks of God's eternity, not a location; He is not bound to one land.

options.text: That God had only recently revealed Himself to Abraham.
options.note: El Olam stresses that God is from everlasting, the opposite of recent or temporary.

quiz.q: How does this small scene fit the larger story of Genesis?

options.text: It shows the promise to bless the nations beginning to bear fruit as a king seeks out Abraham.
options.note: Abimelech's confession echoes the promise of Genesis 12 that nations would be blessed through Abraham.

options.text: It records a minor land dispute with no bearing on the promise.
options.note: There is a dispute, but the scene clearly advances the promise as the nations recognise God's blessing.

options.text: It marks the moment Abraham finally owns all of Canaan.
options.note: Abraham gains only a well and a place; full possession of the land still lies far ahead.

options.text: It shows Abraham abandoning his faith to make a worldly alliance.
options.note: Abraham keeps faith and worships the LORD; the treaty is peace, not compromise.

questions: Abimelech could see that God was with Abraham in everything he did. What would others see about God at work in your ordinary life this week?
Abraham raised his grievance about the well honestly and openly, and it led to peace. Is there a tension you are avoiding that God might want you to address plainly?
Abraham worshipped as a stranger in a land he did not yet own. Where are you being asked to trust and worship God before you can see the promise fulfilled?
Abraham called on God as the Everlasting God. How does knowing that God never changes or grows weary steady you when His promises seem slow?
A pagan king was drawn toward the God of Abraham. Who in your life might be watching, and how could your trust in God open a door for them?

translationNotes.note: Beersheba can mean well of seven or well of the oath; the Hebrew wordplay ties together the seven ewe lambs and the sworn oath, and both senses are in view.
The Hebrew reads El Olam, rendered the Eternal or Everlasting God, a title stressing God's unbounded, age long existence.
